title: Film Handling, Loading, and Processing
narrationScript: Every radiograph you will ever interpret started life as a sheet of raw film in someone's hands in a dark room. Exposure technique gets all the attention, but a sloppy darkroom can destroy a perfect exposure, and the damage usually looks like a discontinuity. This lesson covers how to handle, load, and process film so the only images on it are the ones the radiation put there. Start with the safelight. Industrial X-ray film is far less sensitive to the red and amber end of the spectrum than it is to blue and green, so a darkroom uses a low wattage bulb, typically around seven and a half to fifteen watts, behind a filter matched to the film, mounted at least about four feet from the working surface. None of that makes the light truly safe. It only makes fogging slow. The real question is how long your film can sit under that light before it fogs, and the only honest answer comes from a timed safelight test. Lay a sheet of film on the bench, cover strips of it with cardboard, uncover the strips at timed intervals, then process the sheet and look for density differences between the strips. And remember that film which has already been exposed is several times more sensitive to safelight fog than raw film, so test with exposed film if you want the truth. Check the room itself the same way. Stand inside with all lights out for at least five minutes until your eyes adapt, then look for leaks around the door, the vents, and the pass box. Now the loading bench. A darkroom is split into a dry side and a wet side, and they must stay divorced. Film boxes, cassettes, screens, and hangers live on the dry side. Tanks, chemistry, and anything damp live on the wet side. One drop of fixer flicked onto a loading bench will print a permanent light spot on every film that touches it. Keep the bench clean, keep your hands clean and dry, skip the hand lotion, and handle film only by its edges and corners. Handling matters because the emulsion remembers everything. Bend a sheet sharply over a fingertip and you get a crimp mark, a small crescent that can mimic a crack on the finished radiograph. Squeeze or drag it and you get pressure marks. Slide it quickly out of the box or across the bench in dry air and static electricity discharges through the emulsion, printing black branching tree marks or clusters of dots. So you move slowly and deliberately. Open the film box, open the inner packet, and draw one sheet out gently by the edge. Load it into the cassette against clean lead screens, make sure it lies flat with full screen contact, and seal the cassette completely, because a cassette that leaks light fogs the film edge before the source ever fires. The phrase green film simply means film that has not been processed yet, exposed or not, while the emulsion is still raw and soft. Green film is at its most vulnerable. Keep it cool and dry, keep it away from any radiation source, store boxes standing on edge rather than stacked flat so the weight of a pile cannot press marks into the sheets, and process exposed film promptly because the latent image fades slowly with time. Then comes processing, and the classic manual sequence has five steps. Development comes first, typically about five minutes at sixty eight degrees Fahrenheit, and time and temperature work together. Warmer developer works faster and colder works slower, so you follow the time temperature chart, never your mood. Agitate the hangers when they go in and briefly each minute or so, so fresh developer keeps reaching the emulsion. Next the stop bath, a short dip in dilute acetic acid or a running water rinse, halts development and protects the fixer. The fixer then dissolves the unexposed silver halide and hardens the emulsion. The working rule is to fix for twice the clearing time, which is the time it takes the milky appearance to disappear. Wash long enough in clean running water to carry the fixer out of the emulsion, on the order of twenty to thirty minutes, because residual fixer slowly stains and fades the image in storage. A short dip in wetting agent breaks the surface tension so the film dries without water spots, then the film dries in a clean, dust free cabinet at moderate heat. Replenish the developer as it depletes, keep the tanks covered, and respect the chemistry. The darkroom does not forgive shortcuts, and every shortcut shows up later as an artifact somebody else has to explain.
imagePrompt: A technician in a dim red-lit industrial darkroom lifts a stainless steel film hanger out of a developer tank above a row of open processing tanks, with a glowing interval timer and a clean loading bench with film cassettes visible behind him.

sections.heading: Safelights Are Not Safe, Only Slow
sections.body:
Industrial X-ray film is most sensitive to blue and green light and far less sensitive to red and amber, so the darkroom is lit by a **safelight**: a low-wattage bulb (typically 7.5–15 W) behind a filter matched to the film's spectral sensitivity, mounted at least about **4 ft** from the working surface. Understand what that buys you. A safelight does not make light safe — it makes fogging **slow**. Leave film out long enough under any safelight and it will fog.

Two verifications are standard practice:

- **Safelight test.** Place a sheet of film on the bench, cover it with cardboard, and uncover strips at timed intervals (for example 1, 2, 4, and 8 minutes). Process it and read the strips: the first interval showing a density rise is your working limit. Critically, **pre-exposed film fogs several times faster** than raw film — the exposure has already pushed grains partway toward developability — so run the test on exposed film for a realistic answer.
- **Light-leak check.** Stand in the closed darkroom with every light off for at least 5 minutes until your eyes adapt, then look for leaks around the door, vents, pass box, and processor openings. Fix what you find before it prints fog on your edges.

sections.caption: A working darkroom layout with the dry side (loading and handling) physically separated from the wet side (processing tanks), safelight placement, and light-trapped entry.

sections.heading: The Loading Bench: Dry Side and Wet Side
sections.body:
Every functional darkroom is divided into a **dry side** and a **wet side**, and the discipline that keeps them separate is what keeps films clean. The dry side holds the loading bench, film boxes, cassettes, screens, and hangers. The wet side holds the developer, stop, fixer, and wash tanks and everything that touches chemistry. The two never mix: a single drop of fixer flicked onto the loading bench leaves an invisible residue that prints a **permanent light spot** on every film laid over it, and developer splash prints dark spots.

Bench rules that prevent artifacts:

- Wipe the bench daily; dust and grit between film and screen image as sharp specks.
- Hands **clean and dry** — no lotion, no fixer-damp fingers. Fingerprints image as light or dark marks depending on what was on the finger.
- Handle film **by the edges and corners only**, and only with the cassette or carton shielding it from the safelight when not actively loading.
- Keep food, drink, and smoking out — contamination and light discipline both.

sections.heading: Opening Boxes and Packets, Loading and Sealing Cassettes
sections.body:
Open film deliberately. A box of sheet film contains an inner light-tight wrapper; inside, sheets are usually separated by interleaving paper or in individual folders. Open the box and wrapper fully under safelight, draw **one sheet at a time** out slowly by an edge, and re-close the wrapper before you turn to the cassette. Factory-wrapped film packets (film pre-packaged with or without lead screens) are torn open at the marked end with the same slow, edge-only handling — never rip a packet fast in dry air.

Loading: the film goes into the cassette in direct contact with **clean lead screens**, front screen toward the source side, back screen behind. Inspect screens for scratches, dents, and residue — every screen defect images on the film. Close the cassette so the film lies **flat with full screen contact**; poor contact lets image-forming electrons spread and blurs detail. Then **seal it completely**. Flexible vinyl cassettes seal with fold-over flaps or channel strips; rigid cassettes latch. A cassette that leaks light hands you an edge-fogged film after the crew has already broken down the shot — the most expensive kind of darkroom failure, because the exposure has to be repeated.

sections.title: Green film: the most fragile thing you handle
sections.body: **Green film** is film that has not yet been processed — raw or exposed — while the emulsion is soft and every insult records. Handle it by edges and corners, never slide it fast (static marks), never kink it (crescent-shaped crimp marks), never stack boxes flat (pressure marks) — store them **standing on edge**, cool and dry, **away from any radiation source**, and process exposed film promptly because the latent image slowly fades.

sections.heading: Manual Processing: Five Steps in Sequence
sections.body:
Manual processing is a fixed sequence — **develop, stop, fix, wash, dry** — and each step has a job.

- **Develop.** The developer converts exposed silver halide grains to black metallic silver. The standard reference condition is about **5 minutes at 68 °F (20 °C)**; warmer chemistry develops faster, colder slower, so development is always run by the **time–temperature chart**, never by eye. Agitate the hanger on entry and briefly about every minute so fresh developer reaches the emulsion; films developed without agitation come out mottled and streaked.
- **Stop.** A short dip (roughly 30–60 seconds) in dilute acetic acid stop bath — or a 1–2 minute running-water rinse — halts development instantly and keeps alkaline developer from contaminating the acid fixer.
- **Fix.** The fixer dissolves the unexposed, undeveloped silver halide and hardens the emulsion. Working rule: fix for **twice the clearing time**, the time it takes the milky cloudiness to disappear (commonly 5–10 minutes in fresh fixer). Exhausted fixer = long clearing times = films that stain later.
- **Wash.** 20–30 minutes in clean running water removes residual fixer; thiosulfate left in the emulsion bleaches and stains the image over months in storage, destroying the archival record.
- **Dry.** A 30-second dip in wetting agent prevents water spots, then dry in a clean, dust-free cabinet at moderate heat.

sections.caption: The manual processing sequence with typical times and the reference developer condition of about 5 minutes at 68 °F.
Manual processing steps, typical conditions, and what each step does.
rows: ≈ 5 min at 68 °F per time–temperature chart, with agitation
Converts exposed silver halide to black metallic silver
30–60 s dilute acetic acid (or 1–2 min water rinse)
Halts development; protects fixer from developer carry-over
Removes residual fixer for archival permanence
Wetting agent dip, then dust-free cabinet, moderate heat
Spot-free drying without dust or heat damage

sections.heading: When Handling Goes Wrong: Reading the Damage
sections.body:
Bad handling writes its own signature, and an interpreter must recognize it so a handling artifact is never called a weld discontinuity — or worse, the reverse.

- **Crimp (crescent) marks** — sharp kinking of the sheet; small half-moon marks, dark if bent after exposure, light if before.
- **Static marks** — black branching "tree" or dot patterns from sliding film fast in dry air.
- **Pressure marks** — from stacked boxes or hard local pressure on green film.
- **Light spots from fixer splash** on the bench; **dark spots from developer splash**.
- **Screen-defect images** — sharp repeating marks from scratched or dirty lead screens.
- **Fog** — overall or edge density from safelight overexposure, light leaks, heat, age, or radiation storage exposure.

The darkroom standard is simple: slow hands, clean bench, dry-wet separation, sealed cassettes, chart-driven processing. Films processed this way show the weld and nothing else.

figures.title: Darkroom layout: dry side and wet side

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about darkroom safelights is correct?
options: A red bulb of any wattage is safe for industrial X-ray film indefinitely
A safelight only slows fogging; its working time limit must be verified with a timed safelight test
Safelights fog raw film faster than exposed film
If the safelight filter is correct, distance from the bench does not matter
checkQuestions.explanation: No safelight is truly safe — it only slows fogging. A timed strip test establishes how long film can be handled, and wattage, filter, and distance (≥ about 4 ft) all matter.

checkQuestions.question: Why should the safelight test be run on film that has already been exposed to radiation?
options: Exposed film is cheaper to sacrifice for testing
Raw film cannot be developed
Exposed film is several times more sensitive to safelight fog, so it gives the realistic worst case
Exposed film shows fog as light areas, which are easier to see
checkQuestions.explanation: Exposure pushes silver halide grains partway toward developability, so already-exposed film fogs under a safelight several times faster than raw film — and exposed film is exactly what you handle when unloading cassettes.

checkQuestions.question: Black branching tree-like marks or clusters of dark dots on a processed radiograph most likely indicate:
options: Static discharge from sliding film quickly in dry air
Residual fixer left by inadequate washing
Poor film-to-screen contact
Developer exhaustion
checkQuestions.explanation: Static marks print as black branching or dotted patterns and come from rapid sliding of film out of boxes or across benches, especially in dry air. Slow, edge-only handling prevents them.

checkQuestions.question: How should boxes of unprocessed (green) film be stored?
options: Stacked flat so the sheets stay pressed and straight
Standing on edge, cool and dry, away from any radiation source
In the exposure bay so they are ready for loading
Refrigerated next to the processing chemistry
checkQuestions.explanation: Boxes are stored on edge so pile weight cannot create pressure marks, in cool dry conditions, and shielded from radiation — stored film accumulates fog from any ambient exposure.

checkQuestions.question: The developer in a manual tank line is at 64 °F instead of the reference 68 °F. The correct response is to:
options: Develop for the standard 5 minutes anyway
Increase development time per the time–temperature chart, since colder developer works more slowly
Shorten the development time to compensate
Skip the stop bath to make up the difference
checkQuestions.explanation: Development is governed by the time–temperature relationship: colder developer acts more slowly, so the chart calls for a longer time. About 5 minutes applies at the 68 °F reference temperature.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiography Technique Course 5.1–5.6
Typical manual processing practice: time–temperature development, about 5 min at 68 °F
